speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Hi there, I'm Claudia Hammond.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: You're about to listen to another episode of All in the Mind.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Episodes will be released weekly wherever you get your podcasts.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: But, if you're in the UK, you can listen to the latest episodes a whole week earlier than anywhere else, first on BBC Sounds.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: What would you say the difference is between the mind and the brain?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Today we are delving into their complex relationship and how that makes us who we are.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And my guest for the whole show is Catherine Loveday, who's professor of cognitive neuroscience at the University of Westminster.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: How are you?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: I'm good, thank you.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Well, you've brought with you some brand new research on how exactly the brain brings old memories from our pasts to mind.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yeah, I'm really excited by this paper,

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: because it's it's about that critical moment from something being activated in the brain to kind of coming into our mind.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And I think that gateway is so important.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: The activation in the brain, but when at what point do we actually consciously remember something?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And this research gets us just a little step closer to being able to understand that.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: More on our memories later on.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And this mind and brain theme has been inspired by the emails you've been sending us since the start of the series.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: The inbox at all in the mind at BBC.co.uk is jammed with questions and thoughts that have really got us thinking.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So this week we're digging into some of your ideas.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Now, I'm saying that very carefully.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: We are following up on our discussion the other week on knowledge theft.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: when someone steals your idea, so we need to be very clear about where that idea came from.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Later on we'll be hearing what motivates idea stealing and whether it's always intentional or could be unconscious.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Before all that, some other thorny questions about the mind and the brain.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Paul Fox got in touch with a question.

speaker: Paul Fox
text: I know three people who've had a near death experience.

speaker: Paul Fox
text: What do these types of experiences tell us about the difference between the mind and the brain?

speaker: Paul Fox
text: And should the program be renamed all in the brain?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Interesting. Well, there's a lot to think about there, so let's unpack that.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Here's Carly, who told the BBC what happened to her when she had a heart attack at home.

speaker: Speaker 2 (Carley)
text: I was looking straight up at the ceiling.

speaker: Speaker 2 (Carley)
text: And I kept moving closer and closer.

speaker: Speaker 2 (Carley)
text: It wasn't until the ceiling became essentially right up to my nose.

speaker: Speaker 2 (Carley)
text: That I turned around onto my stomach.

speaker: Speaker 2 (Carley)
text: When I turned my body over, I realized I was looking down at myself.

speaker: Speaker 2 (Carley)
text: I ended up seeing this really bright light, this tunnel of sorts.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So Carly was having the sensation of being out of her body during her near death experience.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And these highly individual experiences raise many profound questions.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And since Paul wants to know what they can tell us about the relationship between the mind and the brain,

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: we asked Anil Seth, Professor of cognitive and computational neuroscience at the University of Sussex, an author of being you.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: So, near death experiences involve all sorts of things.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And and can be very, very variable and they often have things like tunnels of light, feelings of of great peace and and so on.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: But out of body experiences can happen in near death experience as well.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And I think what we can say is that when people are having these experiences, certainly there are changes in the brain that correspond to those experiences.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Precisely what those are, I think we know a bit more for out of body experiences than for near death experiences.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So let's take the out of body experiences.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: What do we know about what might be happening in the brain then?

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Well, there are kind of two ways to think about what an out of body experience might mean.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: The first is to take the experience not only seriously, you know, people do have these experiences, but also to take it literally as if it means that your conscious mind has somehow left your body and and gone to a different place.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: This is consistent with the nature of the experience, but it's very hard to make sense of beyond that.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I mean, if you have an out of body experience and you still see things from there, well, how do you do that if you don't have any eyes, your eyes are still where your body is.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: The other way to think about out of body experiences is that the simple, very natural feeling that we always experience things from a first-person perspective somewhere, usually behind our eyes inside our heads.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Instead of taking that for granted, that's something the brain is continually figuring out all the time.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And most of the time it gets it basically right, so we experience the world more or less from where our our heads are.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: But in some cases, it can get it wrong and we experience the world from a different perspective.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And there have been a few experiments where people have tried to induce out of body-like experiences in laboratory situations or or in the clinic.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Then there's one region which seems to be very important for out of body experiences, um, it's sort of where the brain puts different sensory modalities together, it's called the temporal parietal junction.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And it seems to be if activity in that part of the brain is going a little bit strange.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So you're on the operating table, but your brain then takes the best guess of where you are and kind of gets it wrong, gets the wrong perspective.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I'm glad you used that phrase.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Yes, it makes a best guess, and this in my view, anyway, this is what the brain is doing all the time.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: It's just we don't really know or experience that that's what our brains are doing.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: We know, why should we, we don't really need or want to experience what's actually happening inside our neural circuits.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: But when we open our eyes and look around us in the morning, there's a world, it has objects, there are people, there are places, they have colors, they have shapes.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: That's not because there's this sort of transparent window onto the world through our eyes and our ears.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: The brain, I think, is always making a best guess about what's out there, based on prior expectations and beliefs about how worlds work.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And on the sensory data.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And I think the same thing applies to aspects of what we call the self, and one aspect of the self is this thing called the first person perspective, where are we experiencing the world from?

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And so yes, I think that's exactly right, it's another kind of best guess the brain is making, and in some unusual circumstances when on an operating table for instance, potentially.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: That best guess can go a little bit strange.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So it sounds as though most of the time our brains are making a very good job of this best guess and everything feels as if it's joined together and we get a kind of seamless experience of being in the world.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: What can these kinds of experiences tell us about the difference between the brain and the mind as as Paul asked?

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Well, I think they are just different things.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I mean, the brain is what we call the physical object that is this lump of electrified pate in between our ears inside our head.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: It's a physical object, there's neurons, there's all kinds of other things.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: The mind, I think the easiest way to think of the mind is it's what the brain does.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: But it's also something that arguably goes outside of the head.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And a good example for this, I always think is, these days a lot of my memories are in my phone, whether they're photos or phone numbers.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Also, when I'm writing, the act of writing and thinking what to say or write next, that seems to involve a keyboard, a screen, or a pen and a paper.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: So my mind in these cases arguably extends beyond the brain to sort of reach out and encompass aspects of the world.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: I can see how your memory is kind of being a bit outsourced to your phone and a bit of your memory is there in your phone if you like.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: But, but I don't know, when it comes to writing, I mean, the notebook and the pen aren't, they're not your mind, are they?

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Well, I'm kind ofble that they are.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: There's the philosophers Andy Clark and Dave Chalmers wrote this very influential paper what they called the extended mind, and this really is the idea.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: that if we use objects like pieces of paper and and pens to help us think, to think things we wouldn't be able to do otherwise,

speaker: Anil Seth
text: then it's reasonable to call them part of our mind.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So you're in the operating theater, your brain is doing its best guess on where you are, but it gets it kind of wrong and so suddenly your view is from up on the ceiling in the corner looking down at your body.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Where is the mind at this point?

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I'm just getting like, I can't get the pixi song where is my mind out of out of my mind right now.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Um, I think your mind is exactly where it was before.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: As much as it has a spatial location, your mind is what your brain does, and your brain is still in your body on the operating table.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Your conscious experience is of being somewhere else, somewhere outside your body.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And conscious experience is that part of what the mind does that is experienced subjectively that that has a feeling about it.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: The redness of red or or the sense of pain, or the feeling of of just remembering that you've got to buy some new tea bags.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: You sometimes hear people say that the brain gives rise to the mind,

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: if you like, because the mind presumably can't exist without your brain being there.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: Yeah, I think that's kind of right.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I mean, it's very, very tricky and philosophers and psychologist, neuroscientist have argued for for decades about what the right word is to use for how the brain and the mind depend on each other.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I think it's it's clear that if you change the brain, then most of the time you will change the mind.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: And it's probably true that you can't change the mind without some change in the brain going along with it.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: But it's a bit less clear whether it's right to think of the mind as this sort of cloud floating above the brain, you know, in the same way that a pan of boiling water might give rise to a cloud of steam.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I think it's more useful just to think of the brain as this physical object and what it does in conjunction with the body and with the world, well, part of that is the mind.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So to address the final part of Paul's question, and maybe this is more a question for the powers that be at Radio 4 really, should our program be renamed all in the brain?

speaker: Anil Seth
text: I don't think so actually, cuz all in the brain is is very limited to what's inside our skull.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: But the mind reaches out beyond the skull.

speaker: Anil Seth
text: So I think all in the mind stacks up pretty well.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Few, that's a relief.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Many thanks to Professor Anil Seth.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Now Catherine is still with me and I know this is a topic that you've thought about a lot too.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: How do you conceive the relationship between the mind and the brain?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: I think the brain is the mechanics and the the mind as the experience, I guess.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: You know, if we say we want to put something out of our mind, we're not taking away from our brain, we're just removing it from our consciousness.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So maybe these out of body experiences could almost be called out of brain experiences.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yeah, actually, what it reminds me of is a lot of the research around memory. So we've looked at people's memories of childhood and often people are looking down on their memory.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So if I ask you to about something that happened yesterday, you will typically describe it as if you're looking out on it.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: But actually if I ask you to remember something that happened to you as a child, you're more likely to be looking down on it.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And that feels like a real parallel.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: What, so I see like a little a little me if it's me as a child, but if you ask me about, uh, I don't know, whether I got the bus yesterday, then that's from kind of from behind my eyes, from inside my head, my view of the bus.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Exactly. Yeah, which is I think has real parallels with what Annell's described there.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Well, on the topic of memory, you've got some new discoveries for us on how some memories from our lives seem to just pop into our consciousness.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: I mean, it can feel really random, can't it? You're walking along and you you do suddenly remember something from junior school.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yeah, that's always something that really intrigues me, like why something comes into your mind.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And I think what this research is helping us to do is to work out how that happens, we have so much bubbling away all the time.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: How is it that some things get through?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So what do we already know about how these memory traces work?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Well, if I imagine going to see my niece play football yesterday, I can have images of what that was like, I can remember the taste of the hot chocolate, I can hear the sounds of children, and all of this is is activating different parts of my sensory cortex.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And that gets kind of compressed into a memory trace. So when I now recall that now,

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: to describe it to you, I'm reactivating those bits of my brain, the occipital lobe is helping me to have those images and the auditory cortex is allowing me to hear those sounds.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And one of the key findings is that we can get those bits of reactivation without actually having a conscious awareness, without having a conscious memory.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And that's the bit that's really fascinated me.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: That's really extraordinary, isn't it?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yes, it is. And this research is trying to understand how it is about that selection mechanism, why is it why is it that some memories get through and others just stay kind of activating those areas of the brain but don't give us the actual remembered experience.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So what have these researchers who are from Nottingham University found about how a memory does get propelled into our consciousness sometimes?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So in their study where they were trying to get people to remember things, they found that it was true that people had this sensory activation without a conscious recollection. So they saw this activation in the visual areas that suggested that they might be remembering something, but the people did not report a memory.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: In other cases, they saw these things called alpha oscillations, which are kind of rhythmic pulses of brain waves that seem to signal that conscious recollection.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And what they were able to show is that that's kind of the filter, if you like, it's allowing things to come through into your conscious experience.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And so that's a really critical finding. It shows that it's kind of muting the background noise and allowing that memory to come through into your mind so that you have that sense of experiencing it.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And that's really important because otherwise we would just be remembering stuff all the time, we'd be completely inundated, we need this selective filter to allow some things through and not other things.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So what's been worked out is how this happens, how it is that some memories do come to the surface and we we're conscious of those.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Rather than, than why.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: But you seem really excited about this, why is this so groundbreaking for you?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Well, I want to know why, but we can only know why once we know how.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So this marker of conscious experience allows us to understand that part of the remembering process.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So, for example, if if you can't remember something, is it that the memory's got as far as your sensory cortex, but not made it into your consciousness, or has it not even got that far?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Um, so I think that's really important in terms of us recognizing how we remember, but also trying to understand things like memory loss.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And also just understanding unconscious memories. You know, so much of what we do is shaped by stuff that we're not conscious of, but is still activated in our brain and shaping our behavior.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Yeah, so it's all bubbling away under there.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Well, thank you for that, Catherine.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: You are listening to all in the mind. Yes, we're sticking with that title with me, Claudia Hammond.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Now, a couple of weeks ago, we briefly discussed something known as knowledge theft, which is when someone steals your idea.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And this prompted Bridget to get in touch.

speaker: Bridget
text: Hi, Claudia.

speaker: Bridget
text: I have experienced knowledge theft many times in my career and found it infuriating.

speaker: Bridget
text: It was mostly men, but occasionally women also.

speaker: Bridget
text: It was often blatant and the men seemed surprised when I challenged them.

speaker: Bridget
text: I've warned all my PhD students to be wary of it, but it seems very unfair and very common sadly.

speaker: Bridget
text: Students, beware.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So those were examples of brazen barefaced idea theft.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: But then we also got this from Anita.

speaker: Anita
text: Hello.

speaker: Anita
text: I would like to flag that from my experience, people, male and female, take ideas as their own without even knowing they're doing it.

speaker: Anita
text: I think that we're inundated with information from everywhere.

speaker: Anita
text: We're always filtering out and picking up, creaming off as you like, bits of information and if you're busy, if you're stressed, you've got a lot of going on, you don't necessarily know where the bits come from.

speaker: Anita
text: It's unintentional stealing as it were.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Very interesting from Anita there, and it's set us thinking, is knowledge theft always done on purpose or could it be accidental?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So I caught up with Lillian Ellis, who's assistant professor at the Darden School of Business at the University of Virginia in the US.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So, Lillian, what does research tell us about Anita's experience that people maybe take ideas without even knowing that they're doing it? Does the evidence back that up?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Yeah, so there's this really interesting phenomenon called cryptomnesia or unconscious plagiarism.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And the idea there is that people are unintentionally, unconsciously taking ideas from others and assuming them as their own.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And what happens, so it basically comes back to what's called a source monitoring error.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And the idea there is that it's easier to remember information than it is to remember the source.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: It's really easy to remember content or information, but the source is hard to track.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And and what happens is people miscategorize remembering an idea as generating an idea.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: So then an idea pops to their mind and rather than, oh yeah, I heard that somewhere, they think, what a good idea.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And is this something you've been able to document in studies? Can you demonstrate this happening?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Oh, yeah, there have been a host of studies on this phenomenon.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And what these studies tend to find is that when you closely identify with another person or you're closer in relationship,

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: it can actually increase the likelihood of unconsciously stealing their ideas because you so closely associate them with yourself, and so that psychological ownership that comes up when you're remembering an idea, it gets muddy.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: These might be the people who like best as well. These might be your favorite colleagues.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: That's even worse, it seems, stealing from them.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: I kind of broadly conceptualize it as there being three types of idea theft or idea thieves.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Uh those who take completely unintentionally, right? This is the unconscious plagiarism.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: We have the folks who are intentionally like, I want that idea. Doggy dog kind of world, if you will.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And then we have the folks who want to be inspired, want to pursue work, but they don't understand what is acceptable.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Do I really have to give someone else credit if they just vaguely threw this idea out at the water cooler?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And and that's actually the category that gets the messiest.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And so we ran studies looking at idea completeness, right? So from idea generation all the way to execution, at what point are ideas most vulnerable to theft?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And we found that people who self-identified as idea thieves.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And we, you know, we ran a study with um, you know, 400 people, a really significant number, just very comfortably identified as being willing to steal an idea from a colleague.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Really?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: They admitted that.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Oh, you've made me be more wary now.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: This is very conscious.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And so we'd asked them, all right, so at what level of completeness are you most likely to steal an idea?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And we found that knowledge workers, the creators of the idea, expected their ideas would be stolen in later stages of development so that the idea thief could capitalize on as much work as possible.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: However, these self-identified idea thieves preferred to steal ideas in those earlier stages of completion.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And the reason was because they wanted an opportunity to contribute to it in their own way, they want it to be their idea, but they're trying to figure out how to navigate that threshold.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: We heard a couple of weeks ago on the program about the emotional impact of knowledge theft.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And that we really do mind a lot if somebody steals from us, steals the idea from us and it and it feels like a loss.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And presumably if someone does that a lot, they're going to get a real reputation.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Ideas are personal. We closely identify with them, they're, you know, a lot of research shows that they are a reflection of who we feel we are, right?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And when something is that closely tied to our identity, of course we want to protect it, of course it's going to be important that we are recognized as such.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: You know, when folks are running around stealing ideas in the workplace, there absolutely are reputational consequences.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Uh my research shows that it's actually worse to get a reputation for stealing ideas from a colleague than it is stealing money.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Were you surprised?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: When you found that, because that that is staggering because collegue stealing money is pretty bad, isn't it?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: I, it's illegal, right? I know. It's illegal.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Right? And across a series of studies, thousands of participants in the US and the UK consistently rated people who stole ideas as having worse character, so being less trustworthy, being more immoral and unethical than a colleague who stole money.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: The the mechanism behind it I found is that when people steal money, you know, we judge them very harshly, but there's also this question of, well, why did they do that?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Which tempers that negative judgment a bit. When we hear someone has stolen an an idea,

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: my research shows consistently that we just view that person as trying to get ahead, and that is such a salient and visceral reaction to idea theft behaviors that.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: if you can just credit other folks as much as possible, I'd highly recommend it because it's damaging to one's reputation otherwise.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Is there a way of spotting the difference between the different types of ideas theft, you know, whether somebody has blatantly just picked your idea or whether this was, you know, unconscious theft or something that came up in brainstorming.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: I wish, I will commit to collecting more evidence to understand that in my research.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: I mean, whose idea that is will be very tricky, but that's.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: I will credit you indeed knowledge. We like it if we inspire new research.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So can you ever prove whether it was deliberate or unconscious?

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: You can't. You know, in a laboratory setting, we can, you know, reduce the stakes, so there's absolutely no reason why someone would and then we could observe it.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: But in the actual workplace, someone could say it was unconscious, but there's no way of determining that.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Is it possible though that we might have a kind of bias to overestimating our own role in forging an idea, just as, I don't know, just as most people think they're above average drivers, and we can't all be above average drivers.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Do we tend to think, oh yeah, I mentioned something in there, so it must all be down to me.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Absolutely. I mean, we do a lot of work in our heads.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Right, we may share an idea vaguely, right? Like, oh, I've thought about this marketing campaign idea, but really we've done a lot of churning and processing and the idea ties back to, say, childhood or what have you.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: But what ends up happening is, objectively speaking, you know, when you look at what was actually contributed, was a notion, a concept, but to us as individuals, we see it as so much bigger.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: And so that does create this discrepancy between what we feel we've contributed.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: There have to be mechanisms for the desire for more acknowledgement or attribution.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Right? Like, you know, actually that was my idea or, you know, but but that can be folks can respond to that pretty negatively, right? This kind of claim for credit.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: Um and so what we can do is make sure that others are getting acknowledged for their ideas.

speaker: Lillian Ellis
text: So it's really less about sticking up for yourself in those moments, and instead creating an ecosystem or a culture where other people speak up on your behalf.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Lillian Ellis there.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Catherine Loveday is still with me.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So, Catherine, it sounds as though ideas theft can be accidental.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: But I guess it's hard to prove, isn't it?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: It's really tricky.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: I um, was once asked to do a an evaluation of somebody who had theoretically plagiarized some music.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And the parallels were really similar, it was someone quite famous, and the parallels were really similar to a quite an unfamous band.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Uh so they were claiming that it had been stolen.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: But actually it's really, really hard to know whether it was deliberate.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And and in the end, what we were able to do was to prove that it was almost impossible to have remembered that much in one sitting, which was the only time he could have been exposed to that music.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So there was a scientific way to prove it.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: But it's really, really hard.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: People often don't know themselves.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Is there a brain mechanism or maybe a mind mechanism, um, for this concept where people miscategorize whether they've remembered an idea or they've generated an idea?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yes, so the frontal lobes do something called source monitoring.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So they allow us to work out where a memory comes from.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So if, for example, you tell me a story, you might not remember whether it was a friend that told you or whether you read it on your phone or something.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And we can look at this experimentally, so we can give people maybe two lists of words and we can say at the the recognition stage, was that in list one or was it in list two or was it on the left or was it on the right?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And we know that that is largely driven by the frontal lobes and some people are better at doing that than others.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: So some people may not realize what they're doing.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And it takes us back again to that relationship between the mind and the brain, doesn't it?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And what did you think about cryptomnesia being more likely to happen the closer you are to someone?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Well, this is an ongoing issue in our household, I have to say.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: My husband's always saying, whenever we kind of going somewhere or doing something, he'll say it was my idea and I think it was my idea.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So this is not exactly proper idea theft, but it's still counts, doesn't it? Who decided to go to the theater?

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Oh, it does. It definitely counts.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: In my household we we we often play making up batty book titles.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And our very best batty book title, which I'm afraid is not appropriate for the radio.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Uh we both insist that we made that one up.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yeah, so actually the remembering process is really similar to imagining.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: So they use all the same parts of the brain and it's very much the same process.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And I often think about the very famous example of Paul McCartney waking up with yesterday in his mind.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And where has it come from? Has he imagined it? Has he remembered it?

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And actually, the story that I've been told is that he waited many, many months before recording that song or even taking it to someone else because he was so convinced that it was somebody else's and eventually exhausted all possible solutions.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: He was being so careful, which is very admirable.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Have you had experience of your ideas being stolen? You know, we heard earlier about um academia being a place where this can happen.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Yeah, I have.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: There was there was one particular time when I I suggested something in a meeting and it's it was minuted and everything and I was told it was a very poor idea.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: And then about two days later, I found out that somebody else was running with this idea. I was very, very annoyed.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: But if it was all minuted, didn't that mean that, um, I mean, no one could nick it really, because it was written down.

speaker: Catherine Loveday
text: Still, brazenly stole it.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Shocking.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Well, thank you so much for joining us from the University of Westminster today, Professor Catherine Loveday.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And thanks again for all your emails that get us thinking.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Next week we have a special show from the Hay Festival in Wales when we'll be looking at reading as therapy.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And asking what fiction does for our imagination and our well-being. Do join us if you can.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: Thanks for listening to All in the Mind from BBC Radio 4.

speaker: Claudia Hammond
text: And thanks to producer Erica Wright and our studio engineers today, Mary Stone and Su Maya.
